refnamediv.refpurpose: Introduction to Van Der Corput sequence

refsection.title: Purpose
refsection.para: The goal of this page is to present elements of the theory of the Van Der Corput sequence.

refsection.title: Van Der Corput Sequence
refsection.para: Let k≥0 be an integer and let b be a prime number. We decompose k into base b:
where r is the number of digits required in the decomposition.
We can also write this decomposition as the sum:
The radical inverse function is
This can be expanded in:
In this function, the low order digits of k (with low values of i) are in the high order digits of the radical inverse (with large values of powers of 1/b).
The base-b Van Der Corput sequence is
Notice that

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: The following function defines the base-b Van Der Corput sequence.
refsection.programlisting: function r = vandercorput ( i , b ) current = i ib = 1.0 / b r = 0.0 while (current>0) digit = modulo ( current , b ) current = int ( current / b ) r = r + digit * ib ib = ib / b end endfunction
refsection.para: The following script considers the numbers k from zero to 9, decompose them in base 2 and compute the k-th element of the Van Der Corput sequence in base 2.
The previous script produces the following output.
For example, the integer k=6 decomposes as 110 in base 2. Its inverse is 0.011 in base 2, which is associated to the radical inverse value 0/2+1/4+1/8=0.375.
In base 2, the lowest order digits are alternatively 0, 1, 0, etc... This is why the radical inverse in base 2 is alternatively lower and greater than 1/2.
Here is the same script in base b=3.

refsection.title: Halton Sequence
refsection.para: The Halton sequence in s dimensions uses s primes numbers. Although this is not required by the theory, we usually use the s first prime numbers: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, etc...
Denote by p(i) the i-th prime number. The dimension i of the Halton sequence is the Van Der Corput in base p(i).
The following script produces 10 points of the Halton sequence in 4 dimensions. The prime number used here are: 2, 3, 5, 7.

refsection.title: Bibliography
refsection.para: "Monte-Carlo methods in Financial Engineering", Paul Glasserman, Springer, 2003
"Algorithm 247: Radical-inverse quasi-random point sequence", J. H. Halton, 1964. Commun. ACM 7, 12 (Dec. 1964), 701-702
